title: Sidney Lumet at 100: The Essential Movies From an American Master
format: Sean, Amanda, and Chris Ryan mark what would have been Sidney Lumet's 100th year by walking his 45-year filmography one film at a time: the working methods from his book Making Movies, the misfires he shrugged off, and the ten they'd call essential.

segments.heading: Remembering an American Master
segments.note: the book, the documentary, the working style

films.title: By Sidney Lumet
films.note: Nancy Buirski's documentary, effectively a long interview with Lumet cut against clips; the hosts pair it with his memoir Making Movies as the two texts that frame the episode. Streaming on Amazon Prime.

films.title: 12 Angry Men
films.note: His first feature, directed at 33 after Henry Fonda saw the TV teleplay and handpicked him. One jury room, and instantly one of the greatest movies ever made, the template for everything after it.

films.title: Fail Safe
films.note: Cold War nuclear-strike thriller made before Dr. Strangelove; another Fonda-led exercise in building tension inside a closed room. In the Criterion Collection, later remade live on TV by George Clooney.

films.title: Serpico
films.note: Pacino as the honest cop against a corrupt NYPD. The hosts argue it has aged into being underrated, a rejection of Michael Corleone in both look and performance style.

films.title: Murder on the Orient Express
films.note: The Agatha Christie all-star caper: Finney, Bergman (who won the Oscar), Connery, Bacall. Lumet reused the real train compartments pulled from the Belgian archives; a $40 million hit.

films.title: Dog Day Afternoon
films.note: Pacino and John Cazale in the botched bank robbery. 'Attica' was improvised in spirit, and Lumet directed the neighborhood extras to play their own relationships in the crowd.

films.title: Network
films.note: Paddy Chayefsky's media satire: 'Mad as Hell,' the Ned Beatty boardroom speech, Faye Dunaway on fire, Robert Duvall's Frank Hackett. Lumet ramps the surrealism as it goes.

films.title: Prince of the City
films.note: Three-hour corruption epic with Treat Williams, cast without Pacino or De Niro on purpose. The hosts' pick for the best police movie ever made; the last 40 minutes shot in near-darkness.

films.title: The Verdict
films.note: Sean's favorite: Paul Newman's washed-up lawyer, David Mamet's script, and the closing argument shot at a distance instead of in close-up. Arguably Newman's best performance.

films.title: Running on Empty
films.note: Fugitive radical parents on the run, Judd Hirsch and Christine Lahti, and an Oscar-nominated River Phoenix as the piano-prodigy son. What Daniel was reaching for, done straight.

films.title: Before the Devil Knows You're Dead
films.note: Lumet's last film, made at 83. Philip Seymour Hoffman and Ethan Hawke as brothers who rob their own parents, a dime-store premise that curdles into full Greek tragedy. Albert Finney is very good.

segments.heading: The rest of the filmography
segments.note: yellows, reds, and the ones that got away

films.title: Stage Struck
films.note: An early Hollywood assignment the walkthrough passes over quickly.

films.title: That Kind of Woman
films.note: Lumet 'shotgunned into the Hollywood system,' taking on studio stories that don't quite click. Red.

films.title: The Fugitive Kind
films.note: Brando in a Tennessee Williams adaptation (Orpheus Descending) with Anna Magnani and Joanne Woodward. Hugely celebrated, never a big one for the hosts.

films.title: A View from the Bridge
films.note: Arthur Miller adaptation, hard to find; possibly on YouTube. Not in.

films.title: Long Day's Journey Into Night
films.note: His second feature, a three-hour Eugene O'Neill with Katharine Hepburn, nauseating on purpose. Important to Lumet and cited throughout Making Movies, but edged out of the top 10 by 12 Angry Men.

films.title: The Pawnbroker
films.note: Rod Steiger as a Holocaust survivor. An essential pre-New Hollywood text for its portrayal of trauma and survival; the hosts 'yellow' it.

films.title: The Hill
films.note: First of five Connery collaborations, a brutal WWII military-prison drama in Libya, and one of Connery's best performances. Near-essential.

films.title: The Group
films.note: Mary McCarthy adaptation and a sentimental favorite that 'deeply doesn't work,' but they love that he swung at it.

films.title: The Deadly Affair
films.note: John le Carré adaptation with James Mason as a Smiley figure. A pleasant discovery, sturdy, but not in. On Pluto.

films.title: Bye Bye Braverman
films.note: His first real comedy, a George Segal picture about being Jewish in New York. Couldn't grab the tone; comedy wasn't his forte.

films.title: The Sea Gull
films.note: Chekhov with James Mason; unseen by the hosts.

films.title: The Appointment
films.note: Omar Sharif in Paris, from a James Salter idea. Lumet admitted it wasn't good; he just wanted to shoot in Paris.

films.title: Last of the Mobile Hot Shots
films.note: Bewildering Tennessee Williams adaptation: James Coburn, Lynn Redgrave, a Quincy Jones score, James Wong Howe photography. Everything says classic; it doesn't cohere.

films.title: The Anderson Tapes
films.note: Connery heist thriller with Dyan Cannon, a little Hitchcock, a little anti-Bond. Frank Pierson script, very good, but out.

films.title: Child's Play
films.note: Boarding-school mystery with James Mason after Brando dropped out; the air goes out of it with the recast.

films.title: The Offence
films.note: Fucked-up Connery psychological thriller about a cop who kills a suspect he believes is a child murderer. Yellow, as good as some films that made the cut.

films.title: Lovin' Molly
films.note: Larry McMurtry's Leaving Cheyenne as a Texas love triangle: Blythe Danner, Anthony Perkins, Beau Bridges. Lumet, not the bard of Texas. Red.

films.title: Equus
films.note: Peter Shaffer play adaptation about a psychiatrist and a boy who blinds horses. Acclaimed at the time; the hosts find it an overwrought Greek-myth rehash. Red.

films.title: The Wiz
films.note: Motown's all-Black Wizard of Oz with Diana Ross and Michael Jackson. A flop, a little long, but a crowd-pleaser handed down as a cult classic, at minimum a yellow.

films.title: Just Tell Me What You Want
films.note: Comedy with Ali MacGraw and Alan King, Lumet licking his wounds after The Wiz. Red.

films.title: Deathtrap
films.note: Ira Levin thriller with Michael Caine and Christopher Reeve, a Russian-nesting-doll palate cleanser after Prince of the City. 'Ringer green,' a great time, just not essential.

films.title: Daniel
films.note: E.L. Doctorow's Rosenberg-adjacent novel, adapted by Doctorow, with Tim Hutton and Amanda Plummer as the executed couple's children. Agonizing and unlike anything else Lumet made, genuinely moving; not Hall of Fame.

films.title: Garbo Talks
films.note: Anne Bancroft vehicle the hosts didn't get to. Not seen.

films.title: Power
films.note: Underrated political-fixer drama: Richard Gere as a media consultant, an early Denzel Washington, a deep supporting bench. A big flop.

films.title: The Morning After
films.note: Lumet's Jane Fonda collaboration, with Jeff Bridges. Rickety but one of Fonda's last real hits; she was Oscar-nominated. Not in.

films.title: Family Business
films.note: Connery, Dustin Hoffman, and Matthew Broderick as grandfather, father, and son, three great actors you never buy as related. Fine, not in.

films.title: Q&A
films.note: Nick Nolte as a monstrous, evil corrupt cop opposite Armand Assante; Lumet also wrote it. A hard, hard watch that makes Prince of the City look hopeful. Yellow.

films.title: A Stranger Among Us
films.note: Melanie Griffith undercover in a Hasidic community. A stretch, and the first sign the system was getting away from him. Red.

films.title: Guilty as Sin
films.note: Don Johnson and Rebecca De Mornay in a not-quite-erotic thriller. Watchable, but he's no Adrian Lyne. Not in.

films.title: Night Falls on Manhattan
films.note: Andy Garcia and Richard Dreyfuss in a NYC-government corruption story Lumet wrote himself, from Robert Daley's Tainted Evidence. The best of his thin 90s stretch, even if the poster says otherwise.

films.title: Critical Care
films.note: James Spader in a Network-for-healthcare satire that doesn't work, except for the young Jeffrey Wright life-support sequences, shot all in white, which are gorgeous modern art dropped into a bad comedy.

films.title: Gloria
films.note: Sharon Stone in a remake of the Cassavetes film. Feels duplicative; you just don't need it. Red.

films.title: Find Me Guilty
films.note: Vin Diesel as a mobster who defends himself across an endless mob trial; Lumet co-wrote it. A near-comedy, very long and deliberately durational, not for the hosts.

films: Dr. Strangelove or: How I Learned to Stop Worrying and Love the Bomb

Last of the Mobile Hot Shots.title: Last of the Mobile Hot Shots

Just Tell Me What You Want.title: Just Tell Me What You Want
